nativeRange.habitat: Found on steep reef walls, overhangs, cave entrances, and channel faces where strong currents prevail. Melithaea favors shaded, deeper reef zones — typically 10 to 40 metres — where light levels are low and suspended food is plentiful. Colonies anchor to hard substrates such as rock and rubble.

decorPreferences: live rock anchor point with epoxy or putty
area of strong non-laminar or alternating flow
shaded or lower-light zone (cave, overhang, or shadow of rock)
positioned vertically to allow fan to extend naturally into the current
spacing from neighboring corals and anemones

diet.notes: The Red Sea Fan is entirely non-photosynthetic (azooxanthellate), meaning it contains no symbiotic algae and derives zero energy from light. Survival depends on continuous filter feeding. The diet should consist primarily of phytoplankton — live cultures of species such as Nannochloropsis or Isochrysis in the 3–20 µm particle size range are generally considered most effective — supplemented with rotifers, copepod nauplii, Cyclop-Eeze, baby brine shrimp, marine snow, and other small zooplankton. Food should be target-fed or dosed into the water column daily; flow pumps are commonly turned off for 10–15 minutes before and during feeding to allow polyps time to capture food particles. Regular addition of iodine, strontium, calcium, and trace elements to the water is generally advised.

goodWith: other NPS corals with adequate spacing
pygmy seahorses (Hippocampus denise known host in the wild)
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with sweeper tentacles
photosynthetic corals requiring intense lighting (lighting conflict)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by keiyac; 4000x3000. Melithaea flabellifera in situ — large deep-red gorgonian fan, whole colony, sharp, backlit against blue water; best shows the striking red sea-fan look.
Photo by Anders Poulsen (Deep Blue); 640x480. Melithaea ochracea in situ — vivid red fan beside a tan colony with reef fish; strong red coloration, whole fans in frame (lower res).
Photo by Peter Southwood; 1024x768. Melithaea rubra with polyps extended — bright red bushy colony, sharp, vibrant; adds a distinct species/form.
Photo by Kai Squires; 1024x769. Melithaea sp. close-up of branching mesh showing diagnostic jointed skeleton with red nodes; feature close-up, not a whole-colony shot.
Photo by Anders Poulsen (Deep Blue); 480x640. Melithaea ochracea in situ — orange fan with small fish, whole colony; different colour form (lower res).

summary: The Red Sea Fan (Melithaea sp.) is a non-photosynthetic gorgonian coral from the Indo-Pacific, known for its striking red or orange coloration. Unlike most reef corals, it derives no energy from light and instead depends on daily feeding with phytoplankton and zooplankton. It is generally considered expert-level in care.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT: Melithaea gorgonians are non-photosynthetic filter feeders — they belong to a group called azooxanthellate corals, meaning they contain no symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) and derive no energy from light whatsoever. High-intensity lighting is neither required nor beneficial; placing the colony in a shaded, lower-light area of the rockwork is generally advised. This is in direct contrast to most reef aquarium corals and is the most common care mistake made by beginners.

NPS biology: The distinctive deep red or orange coloration of Melithaea is derived from carotenoid pigments in the tissue and from the calcareous spicules (sclerites) in the colony framework, not from photopigments linked to symbiotic algae. Color does not fade from bleaching in the way zooxanthellate corals do, but will fade as a sign of declining colony health.

Feeding: The single most critical point in captive husbandry. Melithaea must be fed daily, or at minimum 3–4 times per week, for long-term survival. Each individual polyp must receive sufficient food. The flow pumps should commonly be turned off for 10–15 minutes before and during feeding to allow polyps time to capture food particles. Suitable foods include: live or preserved phytoplankton (preferred particle size 3–20 µm), rotifers, copepod nauplii, Cyclop-Eeze, baby brine shrimp, marine snow, lobster eggs, and frozen mysis. Live phytoplankton cultures are generally considered the most effective approach. A dedicated NPS dosing system or refugium with copepod production is commonly recommended.

Flow: Strong, non-laminar (alternating or turbulent) water flow is essential — both to deliver suspended food to polyps and to prevent algae and cyanobacteria from colonizing the branches, which is a leading cause of colony death. Constant direct laminar flow may damage polyps. Alternating gyre pumps or tidal flow systems are well-suited.

Algae management: Algae overgrowth on branches should be removed immediately with a soft brush. Cyanobacteria (red slime) is a common threat; a brief 1-minute freshwater soak (same temperature as tank water) is often recommended to remove it before it spreads. Excellent protein skimming and water quality are key preventive measures.

Lighting: Low to negligible (20–80 PAR is acceptable, full darkness is tolerated). These corals tend to extend polyps more fully in dimmer conditions and are commonly more active at night. Placement under overhangs or in the shaded shadow zone of rockwork is generally advised.

Placement: Can be placed anywhere in the aquarium where strong alternating flow is achievable. The colony should be anchored firmly to live rock using underwater epoxy or two-part aquarium putty, oriented to allow the fan to face into the current.

Water quality: Very good to excellent water quality is generally required. Protein skimming, RO/DI top-off, and regular partial water changes help maintain stability. Regular dosing of iodine, strontium, calcium, magnesium, and trace elements is generally recommended.

System maturity: A fully cycled, mature reef system (minimum 6–12 months) with established live rock and diverse microfauna is generally considered preferable. Dedicated NPS reef tanks — with dimmed or absent lighting and continuous phytoplankton dosing infrastructure — are the setup most likely to achieve long-term success.

Tank size: A minimum of 300 L is generally considered the practical lower bound for a dedicated NPS setup housing smaller Melithaea sp., with 500 L or more recommended for larger colony forms.

Depth in the wild: Typically found at 10–40 m depth on steep reef slopes and channel walls, which explains the preference for low light, strong current, and shaded habitats.

Species note: Melithaea is a genus of approximately 40 described species in the family Melithaeidae (order Malacalcyonacea). They are characterized by a jointed, flexible skeleton with alternating calcareous internodes and flexible horny nodes — a trait that distinguishes them from many other gorgonians. The aquarium trade typically sells multiple undescribed or trade-labeled species under the name 'Red Sea Fan' or 'Orange Sea Fan.' All known Melithaea species share essentially identical azooxanthellate husbandry requirements. Melithaea ochracea is one well-documented species noted for having the smallest polyps among gorgonians, enabling effective feeding even at high current speeds.

Ecological note: Melithaea colonies are known hosts for Denise's pygmy seahorse (Hippocampus denise) in the wild, with seahorses mimicking the red/orange coloration of the fan branches.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in captivity has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild, Melithaea species are simultaneous hermaphrodites that reach sexual maturity after 3–5 years; spawning is typically triggered by temperature, lunar phase, and tidal conditions. Colony growth is very slow — typically a few centimeters per year under aquarium conditions. Fragging is theoretically possible but is not practiced in the trade due to the baseline difficulty of keeping this genus alive.

additional.name: Reeflex – Melithaea sp. 01 Species Profile
additional.notes: Melithaea-specific profile. Key data: temperature 22–27°C, minimum tank 300 L, care 'experts only / very hard to keep', feeding daily or 3–4×/week with dust food and frozen plankton, flow strong, origin Kenya. Used as primary cross-check for genus-specific parameters.

additional.name: Reeflex – Melithaea sp. 03 Species Profile
additional.notes: Second Melithaea-specific profile. Key data: temperature 23–26°C, colony size up to 50 cm, minimum tank 500 L, native range Indo-Pacific / Malaysia, care expert, feeding daily or 3–4×/week, NPS confirmed. Colony size max 50 cm adopted. Minimum tank 300 L chosen (500 L from this source used to bias toward larger; practical min 300 L reflects smaller colony sizes seen in the trade).

additional.name: Wikipedia – Melithaea
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Melithaeidae, Order Malacalcyonacea), azooxanthellate confirmation, Indo-Pacific distribution, and ecological note on Hippocampus denise symbiosis. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: Big Blue Diving Khao Lak – Red Sea Fan Species Profile
additional.notes: Wild ecology data. Key data: depth range 10–40 m (optimal 15–30 m), temperature 25–29°C in wild, native to tropical Indo-Pacific including Red Sea, Indian Ocean, Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Philippines. Temperature from this source (25–29°C) is notably warmer than aquarium-hobby consensus of 22–27°C — likely reflecting wild equatorial temps vs. captive-optimal range. Depth range and native region adopted.
